caveats: a: OEWS covers broader NAICS 522200 rather than exact NAICS 522220.
a: Occupation shares do not directly measure task hours or achievable labor removal.
a: Automation potential varies substantially by collateral, ticket size, channel, and existing system maturity.
rho: Published adoption evidence records use or exploration rather than realized labor substitution.
rho: Equipment-finance evidence may not represent auto and other consumer sales-finance firms.
rho: Regulatory and model-risk requirements can delay implementation despite technical capability.
e: No public source measures eligibility within the supplied firm count and EBITDA band.
e: Captive and independent firms have different separability and valuation economics.
e: Industry classification can blur sales finance with leasing, banking, retail, brokering, and portfolio ownership.
s5: Gallup measures cross-industry intentions rather than completed sales-finance transactions.
s5: A portfolio sale or servicing transfer may not be a qualifying control transaction.
s5: Captive divestitures and institutionally owned platforms are not well represented by an owner survey.
q: No source directly measures retention of AI-enabled gross benefits.
q: Benefit capture differs sharply by direct versus dealer channel and by funding model.
q: Credit and interest-rate cycles can swamp operating savings.
d5: Credit balances and originations are nominal financial measures, not constant-quality service quantities.
d5: Auto and equipment evidence covers major but not all sales-finance segments.
d5: The five-year outcome is highly exposed to macroeconomic and credit cycles.
o: No source directly measures the year-five demand share requiring an in-lens operator.
o: Partner-bank and embedded-finance structures blur where the economically relevant operator sits.
o: Operational labor can fall substantially even when the legal and capital-bearing firm remains necessary.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Sales-finance firms acquire borrowers through direct, dealer, merchant, manufacturer, or vendor channels and earn recurring interest, finance charges, fees, servicing income, and sometimes residual or ancillary economics while bearing funding, credit, fraud, collateral, and compliance costs.
lens.excluded_activities: Credit-card issuers, lease-only businesses, depository institutions classified by banking activity, passive loan portfolios and securitization vehicles without operations, and captive finance departments that are neither separable nor externally facing.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms primarily originating and servicing collateralized credit for purchases of vehicles, equipment, inventory, and other goods, either directly to purchasers or through dealers and sellers, including separable externally facing captive-finance platforms.

a.bridge: BLS employment for broader NAICS 522200 shows substantial business-financial and office-administrative work, including loan officers, loan interviewers and clerks, customer service, and collections. ELFA reports equipment-finance firms implementing AI in sales and credit underwriting and exploring document processing. I map application intake, document extraction, routine underwriting support, dealer and borrower service, collection messaging, compliance testing, and reporting to high or partial exposure, then discount field sales, relationship judgment, exceptions, repossession, and physical collateral work. The broader BLS population may overstate mortgage-like administration, while ELFA respondents may understate digitally native auto and point-of-sale workflows.
caveats: OEWS covers broader NAICS 522200 rather than exact NAICS 522220.
Occupation shares do not directly measure task hours or achievable labor removal.
Automation potential varies substantially by collateral, ticket size, channel, and existing system maturity.
a.change_evidence: An exact-522220 task-and-hour study by auto, equipment, inventory, and other collateral segments, separating already automated activity from manual work and measuring verified AI task success, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Sales finance contains substantial language, classification, decision-support, and service work, but credit accountability, dealer relationships, exception handling, collections strategy, and physical collateral processes remain human-intensive.

d5.bridge: ELFA reports 2024 equipment-finance volume growth with gains concentrated in independents and captives, Federal Reserve data show a large continuing consumer-credit base, CFPB documents more than 100 million active auto-finance accounts and substantial monthly originations, and the ELFA Foundation forecasts positive 2026 equipment and software investment. I combine recent stocks, flows, and the forward equipment outlook into modest five-year constant-quality demand growth, widening the band for rates, recession, delinquencies, collateral values, inflation, and segment mix. Nominal balances can overstate real service growth, while embedded digital finance could produce faster transaction growth.
caveats: Credit balances and originations are nominal financial measures, not constant-quality service quantities.
Auto and equipment evidence covers major but not all sales-finance segments.
The five-year outcome is highly exposed to macroeconomic and credit cycles.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar forecast of financed purchases, active accounts, originations, approval rates, balances, equipment investment, vehicle sales, delinquencies, repossessions, rates, and substitution by leasing or bank lending would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Vehicle, equipment, and inventory purchases continue to require financing, supporting durable demand, but the category is cyclical and highly sensitive to borrowing costs, credit performance, asset values, and business investment.

caveats: No public source measures eligibility within the supplied firm count and EBITDA band.
Captive and independent firms have different separability and valuation economics.
Industry classification can blur sales finance with leasing, banking, retail, brokering, and portfolio ownership.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census matching exact-522220 entities to ownership, standalone EBITDA, receivables, funding, licenses, dealer and merchant contracts, servicing ownership, collateral mix, lease versus loan revenue, and transferability would materially change the range.
e.rationale: A genuine operating sales-finance business can fit the lens, and growth among independent equipment financiers indicates an active nonbank population. The eligibility haircut is large because many establishments are captive units, depository affiliates, very small brokers, portfolio vehicles, lease-dominant firms, or platforms whose funding, dealer contracts, servicing, licenses, or credit infrastructure cannot transfer independently.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 522220 ($10,557,684,131) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 522220 ($137,160,138,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2102 (margins.json[52]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Financial Svcs. (Non-bank & Insurance) EBITDA/Sales 21.02%. Note: Damodaran reports 0.00% for banks (EBITDA not meaningful); bank codes inherit this non-bank default and are flagged ESTIMATE like all n_band values.); revenue/employee $1,430,062 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 522220); class <5: 1,469 firms, avg revenue/firm $811,334 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $170,542 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 328 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,450,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $515,037 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 185 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,161,297 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,295,105 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 187 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,009,722 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,206,044 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $76,819,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,147,354 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 160 firms, avg revenue/firm $772,498,150 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $162,379,111 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 372

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five demand share requiring an in-lens operator.
Partner-bank and embedded-finance structures blur where the economically relevant operator sits.
Operational labor can fall substantially even when the legal and capital-bearing firm remains necessary.
o.change_evidence: Operating-responsibility and market-share data for autonomous lending, embedded-finance, bank-partner, captive, and outsourced-servicing models—showing who owns receivables, sets policy, funds loans, bears losses, controls collateral, and satisfies regulation—would materially change the range.
o.rationale: Software and AI can compress origination, service, and collections labor, but an accountable operator still must contract with borrowers and channels, arrange funding, set credit policy, bear or allocate losses, service accounts, manage collateral, comply with adverse-action and consumer rules, and govern models. Embedded or outsourced finance generally relocates these functions to a licensed operator rather than eliminating them.

q.bridge: ELFA reports pressured profitability, mixed volume growth by lender type, and ongoing technology investment, while Federal Reserve data show a large continuing stock of consumer credit held by finance companies. I infer that implemented workflow savings can be retained through better speed, losses, service, and cost, but haircut heavily for funding costs, dealer incentives, competitive pricing, credit losses, vendor expense, compliance, and customer acquisition. Equipment-finance economics may not represent consumer auto or merchant finance, and aggregate credit balances do not directly measure benefit retention.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-enabled gross benefits.
Benefit capture differs sharply by direct versus dealer channel and by funding model.
Credit and interest-rate cycles can swamp operating savings.
q.change_evidence: Firm-level cohort economics linking AI deployment to operating expense, approval speed, fraud and credit loss, dealer compensation, funding cost, pricing, retention, complaints, vendor cost, and contribution margin would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Interest and fee pricing is not mechanically cost-plus, allowing some internal efficiency gains to remain with the lender, but dealer channels, competitors, funding providers, losses, and compliance consume a substantial share.

rho.bridge: ELFA reports active AI implementation in sales and credit underwriting and widespread exploration in documents and underwriting, while the Federal Reserve identifies current or potential banking uses in fraud, document analysis, underwriting, and service. I translate these deployments into five-year adoption across exposed workflows, then haircut for integration, model governance, fair lending, precise adverse-action duties, hallucination, vendor cost, limited clean data, and human review. Equipment-finance respondents may lag digitally embedded lenders, but banking-wide claims may overstate readiness among small specialty firms.
caveats: Published adoption evidence records use or exploration rather than realized labor substitution.
Equipment-finance evidence may not represent auto and other consumer sales-finance firms.
Regulatory and model-risk requirements can delay implementation despite technical capability.
rho.change_evidence: Representative exact-industry production data showing AI coverage by workflow, human-review rates, errors, complaints, fair-lending outcomes, implementation cost, and verified labor hours saved would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Many workflows are already digital enough for AI augmentation, but regulated decisions and communications require explainable, reproducible output, monitoring, and accountable human oversight.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I use that cross-industry intention as an upper-centered succession benchmark, then haircut for the fact that many sales-finance firms are institutionally owned or captive and that funding, licensing, credit quality, and servicing transfer complicate transactions. Owner intentions may overstate completed sales, while the proxy can miss consolidation triggered by funding or credit stress.
caveats: Gallup measures cross-industry intentions rather than completed sales-finance transactions.
A portfolio sale or servicing transfer may not be a qualifying control transaction.
Captive divestitures and institutionally owned platforms are not well represented by an owner survey.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of completed exact-industry control transactions and closures by enterprise size, ownership, collateral segment, receivables, funding transfer, portfolio-only versus operating-company structure, and seller motive would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Privately held specialty lenders can face succession and consolidation pressure, but portfolios and servicing platforms often have transfer value, and many operators are not owner-managed businesses likely to enter a conventional succession sale.

principal_driver: Recurring origination and servicing around durable demand for financed goods creates a large base of document-, data-, decision-, and communication-intensive work.
principal_weakness: Funding costs, credit cycles, dealer economics, and captive ownership sharply limit both clean target availability and the share of technology benefits retained by operators.
